surface.name: Workers CLI edited Hello Worker deployment
surface.selection_basis: Workshop selection policy (2026-07-19): when official docs present peer routes, commit to the fastest and most commonly used documented route. Prefer the vendor's first-listed or recommended quickstart, dashboard/console over CLI when both are peers, and the route with an explicit vendor time claim or fewest documented steps when tied. Selected route: Workers CLI edited Hello Worker deployment. The CLI guide is the official end-to-end code-first route that creates, runs, edits, deploys, and verifies a first Worker. It is a complete candidate, not a platform-wide default over Dashboard, Prompting, Templates, or Playground. Prior surface before policy commit: Cloudflare Workers getting-started router.

alternatives_considered.name: Workers CLI
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is one of four parallel getting-started routes and requires a Cloudflare account and Node.js.

alternatives_considered.name: Workers Dashboard
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a parallel route through a Git provider and deployment from the dashboard.

alternatives_considered.name: Workers Prompting
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It is a parallel agent-assisted route whose path and result depend on an AI tool and the developer's prompt.

alternatives_considered.name: Workers Playground
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: Cloudflare calls it the quickest experiment, but it is an instant preview without the account, project, and deployment journey used by the CLI and Dashboard guides.

alternatives_considered.name: Workers Templates
alternatives_considered.reason_not_primary: It requires choosing among many application templates and does not define one universal Worker outcome.

entry_point.developer_intent: Build the first Cloudflare Developer Platform application after selecting Cloudflare but before selecting an onboarding interface.
assumed_prior_state: Discovery is complete and the developer has selected Cloudflare.
No Workers onboarding interface has been selected.

documented_first_success.official_milestone: The Workers CLI getting-started guide ends when the deployed Worker's public workers.dev endpoint returns Hello Worker!.
documented_first_success.normalized_outcome: The public workers.dev endpoint returns Hello Worker!.
documented_first_success.observable_completion_signal: The public workers.dev endpoint returns Hello Worker!.

boundary_evidence.official_label_or_terminal_state: The public workers.dev endpoint returns Hello Worker!.

documented_first_success.why_this_is_the_boundary: Selected under workshop fastest/most-used policy; terminal taken from the committed official candidate route.

prerequisites.requirement: Select a Workers onboarding route: CLI, Dashboard, Prompting, Templates, or the separate Playground experiment.

A Cloudflare account is required for the CLI and Dashboard deployment routes but not for a Playground preview; Playground deployment routes an unauthenticated developer through first-time user onboarding.
Node.js 16.17.0 or later is required for the CLI route.
A supported Git provider and a template, owned repository, or public repository choice are part of the Dashboard deployment route.

Cloudflare automatically sends an email-verification message after new account creation; verification requires opening the email link and logging in, with resend and alternate-browser retries documented.
Workers Free is the default account plan. Workers Paid is a separate deliberate purchase with a minimum $5 monthly account charge and is not required for the documented first Worker.

primary_path.action: Open the Cloudflare Developer Platform learning path and follow its Workers path.
details: Cloudflare states that its Developer Platform builds full-stack applications with Workers.
primary_path.success_signal: The Build applications with Cloudflare Workers route is identified.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: No wait is documented.

primary_path.action: Open Workers Getting started, which asks the developer to build the first Worker.
details: The page lists CLI, Dashboard, Prompting, and Templates.
primary_path.success_signal: The interface choices are visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The page does not name one choice as the default.

primary_path.action: Select the onboarding interface whose path will define first success.
details: CLI uses C3, Wrangler, local preview, a code edit, and Wrangler deployment.
Dashboard uses a template or Git repository, Git-provider connection, dashboard configuration, build, deployment, and preview.
Prompting uses an AI tool or editor, optionally connects Cloudflare documentation MCP servers, and requires choosing or writing a prompt.
Templates requires choosing among many GitHub starter repositories and an npm, yarn, pnpm, or deploy-button route.
Playground is a no-setup browser experiment with optional raw HTTP testing, logs, sharing, and deployment.
primary_path.input: Desired interface and durability of the first result
primary_path.success_signal: A human has selected one official route.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Without this choice, prerequisites and atomic steps conflict.

primary_path.action: Open Cloudflare's signup page.
primary_path.success_signal: Email and password fields are visible.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: An existing account can sign in instead and continue at Node.js setup.

primary_path.action: Enter an email address and password, then select Create Account.
details: Workers Free is the default; Workers Paid is a separate purchase and is not required.
primary_path.success_signal: Cloudflare creates the account and automatically sends a verification email.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Form or identity validation must be corrected before account creation succeeds.

primary_path.action: Wait for Cloudflare's automatic verification email.
primary_path.success_signal: The recent Cloudflare message appears in the inbox or Spam folder.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If missing, sign in, open My Profile, and select Send verification email.

primary_path.action: Open the verification link and log in to Cloudflare to verify the address.
primary_path.success_signal: My Profile shows the verified label.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If the link is expired or fails, resend it and retry in another browser or private window.

primary_path.action: Install Node.js 16.17.0 or later.
details: Cloudflare recommends a version manager such as Volta or nvm to avoid permissions problems and switch versions.
primary_path.success_signal: The installed Node.js version is 16.17.0 or later.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: An older or unavailable Node.js runtime blocks C3 and Wrangler.

primary_path.action: Run npm create cloudflare@latest -- my-first-worker.
details: The guide also displays equivalent yarn and pnpm commands; npm is selected for this candidate.
primary_path.success_signal: C3 displays the project prompts.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Package download, npm, permissions, or network errors must be corrected and the command rerun.

primary_path.action: Choose Hello World example.
primary_path.success_signal: C3 advances to application type.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Selecting another example changes generated code and the verification text.

primary_path.action: Choose Worker only.
primary_path.success_signal: C3 advances to language selection.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A different application type changes files and deployment behavior.

primary_path.action: Choose JavaScript.
primary_path.success_signal: C3 advances to Git setup.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: TypeScript changes source files and code.

primary_path.action: Choose Yes for Git version control and No for immediate deployment.
details: The guide delays deployment so the Worker can be edited first.
primary_path.success_signal: C3 finishes and reports project files including wrangler.jsonc, src/index.js, package.json, lockfile, and node_modules.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Choosing immediate deployment changes the order and skips the documented edit-before-deploy route.

primary_path.action: Run cd my-first-worker.
primary_path.success_signal: wrangler.jsonc and src/index.js are present.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A different project name requires the matching directory.

primary_path.action: Run npx wrangler dev.
primary_path.success_signal: Wrangler starts the server for http://localhost:8787.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If this is the first Wrangler use, browser authentication must complete; dependency, port, or configuration errors must be corrected before the server stays running.

primary_path.action: If Wrangler opens a browser login, authenticate with the verified Cloudflare account and authorize Wrangler.
primary_path.success_signal: wrangler dev continues and serves the local Worker.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If browser login is unavailable or fails, follow wrangler login documentation and retry wrangler dev.

primary_path.action: Open http://localhost:8787 and verify the generated response is Hello World!.
primary_path.success_signal: The browser displays Hello World!.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: A missing response requires checking Wrangler output, the local port, and generated source.

primary_path.action: Edit src/index.js so the fetch handler returns Hello Worker!, then save the file.
primary_path.success_signal: src/index.js contains the new response text.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Editing a different file or not saving leaves the preview unchanged.

primary_path.action: Reload http://localhost:8787 and verify the response changes to Hello Worker!.
primary_path.input: Saved source and running wrangler dev
primary_path.success_signal: The browser displays Hello Worker!.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: Confirm the file was saved, wrangler dev is running, and the page was reloaded, then retry.

primary_path.action: Run npx wrangler deploy.
primary_path.success_signal: Wrangler reports a deployed workers.dev URL.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: If no workers.dev subdomain or custom domain is configured, answer Wrangler's publish-time prompt to create one; deployment errors must be corrected and retried.

primary_path.action: Open the reported <YOUR_WORKER>.<YOUR_SUBDOMAIN>.workers.dev URL and verify Hello Worker!.
primary_path.success_signal: The public workers.dev endpoint returns Hello Worker!.
primary_path.failure_or_wait: The first workers.dev push can return 523; wait about a minute and reload before diagnosing another cause.

branches.path: Create or sign in to the required Cloudflare account. New-account setup follows the separate account and email-verification branch in this record.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds the authenticated account gate before local Worker creation.

branches.path: Install Node.js. Wrangler requires Node.js 16.17.0 or later; Cloudflare recommends a version manager such as Volta or nvm to avoid permission problems and switch versions.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a local runtime installation and version gate.

branches.path: Choose npm, yarn, or pnpm and run its displayed C3 command to create my-first-worker: npm create cloudflare@latest -- my-first-worker, yarn create cloudflare my-first-worker, or pnpm create cloudflare@latest my-first-worker.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds a package-manager choice while preserving the same C3 project target.

branches.path: Answer the five C3 prompts in order: choose Hello World example; choose Worker only; choose JavaScript; choose Yes for Git version control; choose No for immediate deployment because the guide edits the Worker first.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Makes template, application shape, language, Git, and deployment timing explicit and reproducible.

branches.path: Change into the generated project with cd my-first-worker and verify the C3 output includes wrangler.jsonc, src/index.js, package.json, the package lock, and node_modules.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Moves execution into the generated project and provides a local scaffold check.

branches.path: Run npx wrangler dev from the project directory to start the local development server.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Starts the local Worker runtime before any hosted deployment.

branches.path: If Wrangler has not been used before, allow it to open a browser and log in to the Cloudflare account. If browser login is unavailable or fails, follow the separate wrangler login documentation before retrying wrangler dev.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds first-use browser authentication and a documented non-browser troubleshooting branch.

branches.path: Open http://localhost:8787 and verify the generated Worker returns Hello World!.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Provides the first local execution signal, before the guide's required edit and hosted success.

branches.path: Open src/index.js and replace its response text so the fetch handler returns Hello Worker! instead of Hello World!.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Demonstrates that the generated Worker code is editable.

branches.path: Save src/index.js and reload the localhost page; verify the output changes to Hello Worker!. If it does not, confirm the file was saved, wrangler dev is still running, and the browser was reloaded, then retry.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Provides the documented edit-save-reload verification and its exact retry checks.

branches.path: Run npx wrangler deploy to deploy the edited Worker.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Moves the verified local Worker to Cloudflare's hosted runtime.

branches.path: If no workers.dev subdomain or custom domain is configured, answer Wrangler's publish-time prompt to set one up.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds first-deployment hostname configuration only when the account lacks one.

branches.path: Open <YOUR_WORKER>.<YOUR_SUBDOMAIN>.workers.dev and verify the deployed Hello Worker! response. If the first workers.dev push returns 523, wait a minute or so for it to resolve, then reload the same URL before diagnosing another cause.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Produces the CLI route's hosted terminal signal and preserves the ordered first-subdomain propagation wait.

branches.path: Create or sign in to the required Cloudflare account. New-account setup follows the separate account and email-verification branch in this record.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds the authenticated Dashboard gate.

branches.path: In the Cloudflare dashboard, open Workers & Pages.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Enters the Dashboard surface that creates Workers applications.

branches.path: Select Create application.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Opens the three documented application-source choices.

branches.path: Choose exactly one source: a production-ready gallery template, an existing Git repository in the developer's account, or a public Workers repository that Cloudflare will clone and bootstrap.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Changes repository ownership and initial code while preserving the Dashboard deployment route.

branches.path: Connect the chosen Git provider for the selected repository or bootstrapped source.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds external Git authorization before build configuration.

branches.path: Configure the project in the Dashboard and select Deploy.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Commits the selected source and build settings to hosted execution.

branches.path: Wait while Cloudflare starts and completes the new build and deployment.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an explicit hosted build and deployment wait.

branches.path: Open the provided workers.dev subdomain and verify the deployed Worker preview.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Produces the Dashboard route's terminal hosted preview.

branches.path: Choose a supported agent or editor; optionally connect the cloudflare-docs MCP server and the observability MCP server; choose an example prompt or copy Cloudflare's base prompt; paste it into the AI tool; insert the developer request between the user-prompt tags; and have the tool create the requested Workers application. The result is prompt-specific, and Cloudflare does not demonstrate one universal terminal application on this page.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an AI-tool and prompt choice, with an application shape and execution path that are not shared by the CLI or Dashboard routes.

branches.path: Choose one of the listed Workers starter repositories, then start it with its displayed npm, yarn, or pnpm create-cloudflare command or its Deploy to Cloudflare action. Each template has a different application and may introduce different services, credentials, and terminal states.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Introduces a template and package-manager or deployment choice before a concrete first-success path exists.

branches.path: Use Firefox or Chrome on desktop, open the Workers Playground without authentication, inspect or edit the default Worker and its automatic preview, optionally send a raw HTTP request and inspect console logs, then optionally copy a share link or deploy. Deployment requires account onboarding when not already signed in and produces a unique public URL.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Removes account and local-setup gates for preview, but sharing and deployment create later branches with different completion signals.

branches.condition: A CLI, Dashboard, or Playground deployment requires a new Cloudflare account, account action 1 of 6.
branches.path: Open the Cloudflare signup page.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Begins the common authenticated-deployment gate.

branches.condition: A CLI, Dashboard, or Playground deployment requires a new Cloudflare account, account action 2 of 6.
branches.path: Enter an email address and password, then select Create Account.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Creates the Cloudflare account; its default name is based on the email address.

branches.condition: A CLI, Dashboard, or Playground deployment requires a new Cloudflare account, account action 3 of 6.
branches.path: Wait for Cloudflare to automatically send the verification message to the provided email address.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds email-delivery latency immediately after account creation.

branches.condition: A CLI, Dashboard, or Playground deployment requires a new Cloudflare account, account action 4 of 6.
branches.path: Open the email provider, find the recent Cloudflare message, and check the Spam folder if it is missing.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Requires inbox access and a documented missing-message check.

branches.condition: A CLI, Dashboard, or Playground deployment requires a new Cloudflare account, account action 5 of 6.
branches.path: Open the verification link in the Cloudflare email and log in to Cloudflare to verify the address.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Completes email ownership verification required for certain Dashboard tasks.

branches.condition: The Cloudflare verification email is missing, expired, or its link fails, account action 6 of 6.
branches.path: Log in to the Dashboard, go to My Profile, select Send verification email, and repeat the email-link login. If the link still fails, try it in a different browser or private window, resend again, and check My Profile for the verified label.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds the complete documented resend and browser-retry path before verification can be confirmed.

branches.condition: The new or existing account has not deliberately purchased Workers Paid.
branches.path: Use the Workers Free plan that Cloudflare provides by default. It includes limited Workers, Pages Functions, Workers KV, and Hyperdrive usage; the current Workers allowance includes 100,000 requests per day and 10 milliseconds of CPU time per invocation.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Keeps the first Worker deployment on the default no-purchase route with documented usage limits.

branches.condition: The developer deliberately chooses Workers Paid for increased allotments or paid-only capabilities.
branches.path: Purchase the separate Workers Paid plan for a minimum account charge of $5 USD per month, then use its Standard usage model and paid allotments. Do not treat this purchase as part of default account creation or the Free-plan first deployment.
branches.effect_on_first_success: Adds an explicit paid subscription and billing boundary that is conditional, not automatic.

friction_gates.description: The official Workers router requires an interface choice that changes the entire first-mile path.

CLI and Dashboard deployments require a Cloudflare account; Playground preview does not, but deploying from Playground requires login or first-time onboarding. New account creation asks for email and password and automatically starts email verification.
The CLI path requires Node.js and installs Wrangler through C3.
The Prompting route requires choosing an AI tool and prompt, and the Templates route requires choosing a starter repository and launch method.
The CLI guide states that first-time workers.dev 523 errors may require waiting a minute or so to resolve, then reloading the deployed URL.
New accounts receive an automatic verification email; missing or expired messages require Dashboard resend, and failing links can require another browser or private window.
The CLI route requires npm, yarn, or pnpm plus exact C3 choices for example, Worker-only template, JavaScript, Git, and deferred deployment.
The Dashboard route waits for Cloudflare's hosted build and deployment before the workers.dev preview exists.
Workers Free is the default plan; Workers Paid is a separate optional purchase with a minimum $5 monthly account charge.

sources.title: Learn about Cloudflare's Developer Platform
sections_used: Build applications with Cloudflare's Developer Platform
evidence_supported: Workers as the full-stack application surface of Cloudflare Developer Platform

sources.title: Workers Getting started
evidence_supported: Parallel CLI, Dashboard, Prompting, and Templates routes

sources.title: Workers Get started: Dashboard
evidence_supported: Template, owned repository, and cloned public repository choices

sources.title: Workers Get started: Prompting
evidence_supported: Absence of one prompt-independent terminal application

sources.title: Workers Get started: Templates
evidence_supported: npm, yarn, pnpm, and Deploy to Cloudflare launch options

sources.title: Workers Playground
evidence_supported: Firefox and Chrome desktop requirement and Safari failure

sources.title: Verify email address
evidence_supported: Inbox, Spam, link, and login actions

uncertainties.question: Should Cloudflare first success be a no-setup Playground preview, an agent-generated application, a chosen template, a locally edited Worker, or a deployed workers.dev Worker?
uncertainties.impact: The answer changes browser support, account, software, AI-tool, prompt, template, Git, authentication, code-edit, deployment, and wait gates.
uncertainties.reason_unresolved: The official Workers getting-started page presents CLI, Dashboard, Prompting, and Templates as parallel routes, and the Dashboard and Playground pages separately call Playground the quickest experiment without making it the universal first path.

excluded_after_success: Completing any one interface-specific Worker route until a human chooses the canonical route
Bindings, examples, tutorials, testing, debugging, custom domains, and automatic builds after the first Worker preview
